title: AGY-431: Scale & Immutable DevOps (L400)
description: An advanced, hands-on masterclass for DevOps Leads and Platform Engineers. Master the architecture, deployment, and orchestration of containerized Google Antigravity environments (agy-box) across remote cloud VMs. Learn how to configure private container registries behind restrictive corporate proxies, manage TLS/SSL certificate injection, and implement secure headless CI/CD execution loops with strict policy enforcement. This course features step-by-step guidance on setting up, configuring, and authenticating Google Workspace MCP services across multiple deployment surfaces.

highlights.title: Immutable Sandboxing
highlights.description: Establish bulletproof isolation for autonomous agents using hardened OCI agy-box containers with read-only filesystems and capability drops.

highlights.title: Enterprise Proxy Integration
highlights.description: Master certificate injection, HTTP/HTTPS proxy routing, and authentication bypass rules for air-gapped corporate environments.

highlights.title: Headless CI/CD Orchestration
highlights.description: Build automated, non-interactive execution pipelines with the agy CLI, custom timeout handlers, and structural status checks.

blocks.content: The Paradigm of Immutable Agent Isolation

Traditional agent deployments execute scripts directly on the host virtual machine, presenting an unacceptable risk profile in enterprise environments. An autonomous agent with shell access can perform destructive commands, compromise host credentials, or escalate privileges. In the Antigravity architecture, we enforce Tier 3 Sandboxing using the `agy-box` OCI image. This ensures that the engine runs in an ephemeral, containerized environment, separating the agent's execution context from the host VM's kernel and metadata server.

The agy-box image is built on a minimal, hardened base distro (typically Alpine or Wolfi) and runs as a rootless container. It strips unnecessary kernel capabilities, enforces strict read-only root filesystems, and locks the agent workspace to a single mapped volume.
Rootless Podman vs. Docker Sandboxing

In high-security enterprise environments, rootless Podman is the preferred container runtime. Since Podman does not rely on a root-owned daemon, it leverages Linux user namespaces (userns) to map the container's root user to an unprivileged UID on the host VM. This prevents container escape vulnerabilities from compromising the host.

# Verify host support for user namespaces
sysctl user.max_user_namespaces

# Create an unprivileged system user for agy-box execution
sudo useradd -r -s /usr/sbin/nologin agy_runner

# Configure subuid and subgid mappings for rootless container execution
echo "agy_runner:100000:65536" | sudo tee -a /etc/subuid
echo "agy_runner:100000:65536" | sudo tee -a /etc/subgid

# Run a diagnostic container to verify rootless execution mapping
sudo -u agy_runner podman run --rm alpine whoami
Firewalling & Network Egress Restrictions

AI agents must be restricted to prevent data exfiltration. The agy-box container should only have egress access to approved API endpoints (such as Google Vertex AI, GCP APIs, and private registries) while blocking access to internal databases, metadata servers, and other VMs.

# Deny container access to the GCP Instance Metadata Server (169.254.169.254)
# This prevents the agent from harvesting host service account tokens.
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -d 169.254.169.254 -j REJECT

# Launch the agy-box container with specific network controls
podman run -d \
  --name agy-sandbox \
  --network slirp4netns:port_forward=8080 \
  --cap-drop=ALL \
  --security-opt label=disable \
  --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp \
  -v /opt/agy/workspace:/workspace:z \
  gcr.io/tridorian-corp/agy-box:v1.2.0
Google Workspace Developer Preview Program Enrollment

To use the official Google Workspace MCP servers, you must enroll your developer account and GCP projects in the Google Workspace Developer Preview Program. This public program grants access to the dedicated Workspace Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, which expose safe, high-level endpoints for agent interaction without requiring custom API integration code. You can sign up and ensure your account is accepted into the program at: https://developers.google.com/workspace/preview.
Enabling Standard Workspace APIs & MCP Services

To use Google Workspace MCP servers, you must enable the standard Google Workspace APIs and the corresponding Google Workspace MCP Services in your GCP project. Note that some MCP servers operate independently, while others require the standard API to be enabled:

items: Gmail and Chat: Require both their standard APIs and their MCP services to be enabled (gmail.googleapis.com, chat.googleapis.com, gmailmcp.googleapis.com, chatmcp.googleapis.com).
Drive: Requires the standard API for certain tools and the Drive MCP Service (drive.googleapis.com, drivemcp.googleapis.com).
Calendar: Does not require the standard API; only requires the Calendar MCP Service to be enabled (calendarmcp.googleapis.com).
People API: Handles both standard access and MCP functionality, so it does not have a separate MCP service to enable (people.googleapis.com).

blocks.content:
# Enable standard Google Workspace APIs
gcloud services enable \
  chat.googleapis.com \
  drive.googleapis.com \
  gmail.googleapis.com \
  people.googleapis.com

# Enable Google Workspace MCP Services
gcloud services enable \
  calendarmcp.googleapis.com \
  chatmcp.googleapis.com \
  drivemcp.googleapis.com \
  gmailmcp.googleapis.com
Check Your Understanding
Question 1: Which Google Workspace MCP service is independent and does NOT require its standard counterpart API to be enabled in the Google Cloud Console?
A) Gmail MCP (gmailmcp.googleapis.com)
B) Calendar MCP (calendarmcp.googleapis.com)
C) Chat MCP (chatmcp.googleapis.com)
D) People API (people.googleapis.com)
Correct Answer: B. Google Calendar MCP operates independently and does not require the standard Google Calendar API to be enabled in the GCP project.
Question 2: Why must the IP address 169.254.169.254 be explicitly blocked in the output iptables rules of the sandbox container?
A) It is the default Gateway for private registries.
B) It is the cloud provider metadata service IP, and blocking it prevents the agent from harvesting host service account tokens.
C) It is the URL redirect callback endpoint for OAuth consent.
D) It represents the external proxy server IP.
Correct Answer: B. The GCP metadata server (169.254.169.254) distributes host credentials, which must be blocked to enforce complete sandbox isolation.
Navigating Restrictive Corporate Networks

Enterprise environments route all external traffic through forward proxies (such as Squid or Zscaler) that perform SSL interception. To deploy agy-box reliably, you must configure the container runtime to trust corporate certificates and route image pull and push requests through the appropriate proxies.

Failing to inject corporate Root CA certificates into both the host container daemon and the agy-box image itself will result in immediate SSL handshake failures (x509: certificate signed by unknown authority) when communicating with public or private APIs.
Injecting Custom SSL Certificates
To ensure the engine can download dependencies and connect to LLM endpoints, the enterprise Root CA certificate must be copied into the container during the build stage.

# Custom Dockerfile extending the base agy-box image
FROM gcr.io/tridorian-corp/agy-box:v1.2.0

# Switch to root to import certificates
USER root

# Copy corporate ROOT CA certificate
COPY certs/corp-root-ca.crt /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/corp-root-ca.crt

# Update certificates database
RUN chmod 644 /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/corp-root-ca.crt && \
    update-ca-certificates

# Revert to unprivileged runner user
USER agy_runner
Registry Setup & Proxy Configurations
Configure the container engine (Podman/Docker) to use the corporate proxy for pulling images, while bypassing the proxy for internal private registries.

# Create systemd override directory for Podman/Docker
sudo mkdir -p /etc/systemd/system/podman.service.d/

# Define proxy environment variables in systemd override configuration
cat <<EOF | sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/podman.service.d/http-proxy.conf
[Service]
Environment="HTTP_PROXY=http://proxy.corp.internal:8080"
Environment="HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy.corp.internal:8080"
Environment="NO_PROXY=localhost,127.0.0.1,private-registry.corp.internal,169.254.169.254"
EOF

# Reload systemd daemon and restart the container service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart podman

# Authenticate to the private registry using corporate credentials
podman login private-registry.corp.internal \
  --username agy-deployer \
  --password-stdin < /etc/secrets/registry-token.txt
Google Chat App Setup
To enable the Google Chat MCP server to search and read messages, you must register a Google Chat app configuration in your GCP project:

items: Search for "Google Chat API" in the GCP Console search bar, and select Manage > Configuration.
Set the App Name field to "Chat MCP".
Set the Avatar URL field to "https://developers.google.com/chat/images/quickstart-app-avatar.png".
Set the Description field to "Chat MCP server".
Under Functionality, turn off "Enable interactive features" (as our agents run as background services).
Under Logs, select "Log errors to Logging" and click Save.

blocks.content: OAuth Consent branding and Granular Scopes
Google Workspace MCP servers require OAuth 2.0. In the Google Auth Platform portal, configure the OAuth Consent Screen and add the necessary scopes for each Workspace server:

items: Navigate to Google Auth Platform > Branding, and click Get Started.
Set App Name to "Google Workspace MCP Servers" and select your user support email.
Configure the Audience to Internal (restricts access to users in your organization) or External (requires adding your email under Test Users).
Under Contact Information, enter your developer email, then review and click Create.
Navigate to Data Access > Add or Remove Scopes, scroll to the bottom, and manually add the following scopes under "Manually add scopes":

blocks.content:
Google Calendar:
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.calendarlist.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.events.freebusy
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar.events.readonly

Google Chat:
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.spaces.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.memberships.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.messages.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.users.readstate.readonly

Google Drive:
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.file

Gmail:
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.compose

People API:
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/directory.readonly
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/userinfo.profile
https://www.googleapis.com/auth/contacts.readonly
Creating Web Application Client Credentials

With scopes registered, you must generate credentials. Since Antigravity interfaces coordinate the login flow on behalf of the developer, you must create a Web application client with an exact authorized redirect URI:

items: Go to Google Auth Platform > Clients > Create Client.
Select Web application as the application type.
Name the client "Antigravity".
Under Authorized redirect URIs, click "+ Add URI" and enter exactly: https://antigravity.google/oauth-callback
Click Create, and copy the generated Client ID and Client Secret to a secure configuration repository.

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding
Question 1: What is the exact redirect URI that must be registered under the Web application credentials for Antigravity Workspace MCP integration?
A) https://antigravity.google/oauth-callback
B) http://localhost:8080/callback
C) https://developers.google.com/oauth-callback
D) urn:ietf:wg:oauth:2.0:oob
Correct Answer: A. The authorized redirect URI must be set exactly to https://antigravity.google/oauth-callback to align with Antigravity's OAuth flow.
Question 2: Which scope allows the Google Chat MCP server to search and read messages in space chats?
A) https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.spaces.readonly
B) https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.messages.readonly
C) https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.bot
D) https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.admin
Correct Answer: B. The https://www.googleapis.com/auth/chat.messages.readonly scope is required to search and read messages within space chats.
Automating Infrastructure for Agent Workloads

Scaling agent workflows requires automated VM provisioning. We use Terraform to define the target virtual machines, secure firewall rules, and metadata scripts that bootstrap the host, mount directories, configure systemd, and launch the agy-box container.
Terraform Configuration for VM Bootstrapping
The following Terraform manifest provisions a hardened GCE instance with metadata that automatically configures Podman and deploys the custom agy-box image.

resource "google_compute_instance" "agy_sandbox_vm" {
  name         = "agy-sandbox-vm-prod"
  machine_type = "e2-standard-4"
  zone         = "us-central1-a"

  boot_disk {
    initialize_params {
      image = "cos-cloud/cos-stable" # Container-Optimized OS
      size  = 50
    }
  }

  network_interface {
    network    = "corp-vpc"
    subnetwork = "secure-agents-subnet"
    # Omit access_config to ensure no public IP is assigned
  }

  metadata = {
    user-data = <<-EOF
      #cloud-config
      write_files:
      - path: /etc/systemd/system/agy-box.service
        permissions: "0644"
        owner: root
        content: |
          [Unit]
          Description=Google Antigravity Sandbox OCI Container
          After=network-online.target
          Wants=network-online.target
          [Service]
          Restart=always
          ExecStartPre=-/usr/bin/docker kill agy-sandbox
          ExecStartPre=-/usr/bin/docker rm agy-sandbox
          ExecStart=/usr/bin/docker run \
            --name agy-sandbox \
            --read-only \
            -v /var/opt/agy/workspace:/workspace:rw \
            -p 8080:8080 \
            gcr.io/tridorian-corp/agy-box:v1.2.0
          ExecStop=/usr/bin/docker stop agy-sandbox
          [Install]
          WantedBy=multi-user.target
    EOF
  }

  service_account {
    email  = "agy-runner-sa@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com"
    scopes = ["https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform"]
  }
}
Configuring Host systemd Service Managers

In standard Linux distributions (like RedHat, Ubuntu, or Debian), we use systemd services to guarantee high availability of the sandboxed agent server. This setup ensures that if the agent process crashes or the VM reboots, the environment is automatically rebuilt in its clean, immutable state.

# Location: /etc/systemd/system/agy-sandbox.service
[Unit]
Description=AGY Containerized Engine Service
After=network.target network-online.target podman.socket
Requires=podman.socket

[Service]
Type=simple
User=agy_runner
Group=agy_runner
WorkingDirectory=/opt/agy
ExecStartPre=-/usr/bin/podman kill agy-sandbox
ExecStartPre=-/usr/bin/podman rm agy-sandbox
ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman run \
  --name agy-sandbox \
  --cap-drop=ALL \
  --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp \
  -v /opt/agy/workspace:/workspace:z \
  -e AGY_SECRET_TOKEN_FILE=/etc/secrets/token.env \
  private-registry.corp.internal/agy/agy-box:latest
ExecStop=/usr/bin/podman stop agy-sandbox
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5s

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
Workspace MCP Server Setup in mcp_config.json

Antigravity 2.0, the IDE, and the CLI share a central configuration file at ~/.gemini/config/mcp_config.json. Modify this configuration file to register the five Google Workspace MCP servers. Replace client ID and client secret placeholders with the Web client credentials created in Module 2:
Authenticating Custom MCP Servers Across Interfaces
Once mcp_config.json is saved, the configured servers will show as installed. Since OAuth tokens are managed locally on each surface, you must authenticate the servers on each interface you use:
Surface 1: Antigravity 2.0 (Mission Control GUI)

items: Click Settings in the bottom-left corner of the window.
Navigate to the Customizations tab.
Under Installed MCP Servers, click Refresh.
For each server (gws-calendar, gws-chat, gws-drive, gws-gmail, gws-people), click Authenticate.
In the browser window that opens, sign in and accept scopes, click "Copy to Clipboard", then paste the auth code back into Antigravity 2.0 settings and click Submit. A "Sign out" button will appear.

blocks.content: Surface 2: Antigravity IDE (VS Code Fork)

items: Open MCP Servers via the "..." dropdown at the top of the side agent panel, or click "Manage MCP Servers".
Open Antigravity User Settings via the Editor-Specific settings menu dropdown at the top of the window.
Go to Customizations, click Refresh under Installed MCP Servers.
Click Authenticate for each server, complete the browser authorization flow, copy the auth code, paste it into the IDE settings field, and click Submit.

blocks.content: Surface 3: Antigravity CLI (agy)

items: In your terminal, execute the command: /mcp
Use the up/down arrows to select a server (e.g., gws-gmail) and press Enter.
Use the left/right arrows to select Authenticate and press Enter.
Open the displayed OAuth URL in your browser, log in and consent, copy the authorization code.
Paste the code directly into the terminal prompt next to the "Enter code:" text field and press Enter. Verify the status updates to "Authed".

blocks.content: Check Your Understanding
Question 1: Why must we run the authentication flow separately on the CLI, the IDE, and the Standalone GUI interfaces?
A) The redirect URI is different for each interface.
B) OAuth tokens are stored in the local application storage directories unique to each execution surface.
C) Google Cloud allows only one active connection per application type.
D) The client credentials must be refreshed manually every time.
Correct Answer: B. Although the client credentials are the same, the actual OAuth access/refresh tokens are stored in separate directories managed by the CLI, IDE, and Standalone GUI.
Question 2: Which file stores the OAuth client ID and secret required for Workspace MCP integration?
A) /etc/secrets/token.env
B) ~/.gemini/config/mcp_config.json
C) ~/.config/antigravity/tokens.json
D) .gemini.md
Correct Answer: B. The ~/.gemini/config/mcp_config.json file configures all MCP servers and their matching client ID and client secret fields.
Executing Agent Workloads in CI/CD

To scale agentic engineering, agent loops must run unattended in continuous integration pipelines. A headless run executes without user interaction, reading task instructions, running files, performing checks, and reporting results. This requires configuring non-interactive authorization, defining timeout thresholds, and capturing build artifacts.

Because headless pipelines cannot request human-in-the-loop approvals, any CLI invocation must include the --auto-approve flag or a custom workspace rule file (.gemini.md) specifying permitted actions. Ensure strict repository branch protections are active so agents cannot run unreviewed code changes.
Pre-Authenticating and Injecting OAuth Tokens

Since headless execution environments cannot prompt for interactive browser sign-ins, you must pre-authenticate the Workspace MCP servers on a secure workstation. Extract the generated token cache files, encode them, and inject them into the target container environment at runtime.

# On your secure workstation, run /mcp to authenticate all servers.
# Locate the generated credentials directory (usually ~/.config/antigravity/tokens)
# Package the credentials into a base64 string
tar -czf - -C ~/.config/antigravity/tokens . | base64 -w 0 > gws-tokens-base64.txt

# Save the contents of gws-tokens-base64.txt as a protected CI/CD secret variable: GWS_AUTH_TOKENS_BASE64
Production-Grade GitLab CI/CD Configuration
The following GitLab CI/CD snippet runs a headless agent sequence inside an ephemeral agy-box workspace, processing a pull request, querying Google Workspace, and generating a report.

stages:
  - analyze

run_headless_agent:
  stage: analyze
  image: private-registry.corp.internal/agy/agy-box:latest
  variables:
    AGY_WORKSPACE_DIR: "$CI_PROJECT_DIR"
    AGY_HEADLESS: "true"
    AGY_API_KEY: "$AGY_PROD_API_KEY"
  before_script:
    # Setup isolated temporary directory within workspace
    mkdir -p $CI_PROJECT_DIR/.tmp
    export TMPDIR=$CI_PROJECT_DIR/.tmp
    
    # Restore pre-authenticated Google Workspace tokens
    mkdir -p /home/agy_runner/.config/antigravity/tokens
    echo "$GWS_AUTH_TOKENS_BASE64" | base64 -d | tar -xzf - -C /home/agy_runner/.config/antigravity/tokens
    
    # Verify API authorization credentials
    agy --version
    agy auth verify --token $AGY_API_KEY
  script:
    # Execute the agent loop on the workspace.
    # --auto-approve allows executing commands without human prompts.
    # --timeout 600 limits execution time to 10 minutes.
    agy run \
      --task "Search Drive for 'release-notes-v2', read and summarize their contents, find the email for release-manager in Directory, and create a Gmail draft to them with the summary." \
      --auto-approve \
      --timeout 600 \
      --rules-file .gemini.md
  artifacts:
    name: agent-changes
    paths:
      - agy-report.json
    expire_in: 7 days
Advanced CI/CD Pipeline Automation (GitHub Actions)
GitHub Actions can also run headless agent operations. We use actions secrets to pass API keys securely and checkout only the specific pull request branch.

name: Headless Agent Automation
on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'src/**'

jobs:
  run-agent:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout Repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v3
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
          fetch-depth: 0
      - name: Restore Pre-Authenticated Workspace Tokens
        run: |
          mkdir -p $HOME/.config/antigravity/tokens
          echo "${{ secrets.GWS_AUTH_TOKENS_BASE64 }}" | base64 -d | tar -xzf - -C $HOME/.config/antigravity/tokens
      - name: Run Ephemeral AGY-Box Sandbox
        run: |
          docker run --rm \
            -v ${{ github.workspace }}:/workspace \
            -v $HOME/.config/antigravity/tokens:/home/agy_runner/.config/antigravity/tokens \
            -e AGY_API_KEY=${{ secrets.AGY_API_KEY }} \
            -e GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS=/workspace/gcp-creds.json \
            private-registry.corp.internal/agy/agy-box:latest \
            agy run \
              --task "Check the Google Calendar for any event overlaps tomorrow, look for messages containing 'blocker' in Chat Space 'incident-response', and email the summaries to the team via Gmail." \
              --auto-approve \
              --workspace /workspace \
              --output-report /workspace/agy-report.json
      - name: Commit Agent Suggestions
        run: |
          git config --global user.name "AGY Headless Runner"
          git config --global user.email "agy-runner@corp.internal"
          git add src/
          git diff-index --quiet HEAD || git commit -m "style: automated agent security refactoring"
          git push origin HEAD:${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
Check Your Understanding
Question 1: What is the purpose of passing the GWS_AUTH_TOKENS_BASE64 secret into the headless container's config directory?
A) To bypass the corporate proxy checks.
B) To inject pre-authenticated Google Workspace OAuth tokens so the agent can execute tasks without interactive browser dialogs.
C) To authorize systemd to start Podman without root privileges.
D) To register the redirect URI callback endpoint.
Correct Answer: B. Restoring pre-authenticated token cache files bypasses the interactive login requirement, enabling headless pipelines to authenticate to Google Workspace MCP servers.
Question 2: Which flag must be provided to the agy CLI during a headless run to prevent the pipeline from hanging indefinitely?
A) --verbose
B) --auto-approve
C) --no-sandbox
D) --headless
Correct Answer: B. The --auto-approve flag tells the agent to proceed with command executions without waiting for user confirmation in the terminal.
The Platform Engineer Capstone Challenge

The final assessment for the L400 Platform Engineer credential is a comprehensive, hands-on integration test. You must build, configure, deploy, and verify a complete, containerized agentic development pipeline. This capstone is designed to simulate a real-world enterprise infrastructure deployment under proxy and network constraints.
Overview of Capstone Deliverables
To successfully pass this challenge and earn the Certified AGY Platform Engineer badge, you must deliver:

items: A custom agy-box Dockerfile containing corporate proxy settings and corporate CA certificates.
An automated deployment script using Podman and systemd on a mock remote cloud VM.
A functional mcp_config.json containing the five Google Workspace MCP servers (gws-calendar, gws-chat, gws-drive, gws-gmail, gws-people) along with Web client credentials.
A functional GitLab CI/CD or GitHub Actions workflow configuration that restores pre-authenticated OAuth tokens and executes a headless agy run in --auto-approve mode.
A verified .gemini.md rules schema restricting the agent from running any commands matching destructive patterns (e.g., rm -rf /).

blocks.content: Step-by-Step Verification Script

Use the following local shell script to verify your capstone components before submitting for grading. The verification tool checks container status, mounts, proxy variables, mcp_config.json values, and runs a diagnostic CLI check verifying Workspace MCP server connectivity.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# verification.sh - L400 Capstone Validation Tool
set -euo pipefail

echo "==========================================="
echo "🔍 Running AGY-431 Capstone Verification Script"
echo "==========================================="

# 1. Check container execution status
if ! podman ps | grep -q "agy-sandbox"; then
  echo "❌ Error: Podman container 'agy-sandbox' is not running!"
  exit 1
else
  echo "✓ Container 'agy-sandbox' is running."
fi

# 2. Check proxy settings in the container environment
PROXY_ENV="$(podman exec agy-sandbox env | grep -E "HTTP_PROXY|HTTPS_PROXY" || true)"
if [ -z "$PROXY_ENV" ]; then
  echo "⚠️ Warning: No HTTP/HTTPS proxy configuration detected in the container!"
else
  echo "✓ Proxy variables mapped: "
  echo "$PROXY_ENV"
fi

# 3. Check workspace directory mount
if ! podman exec agy-sandbox ls /workspace >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "❌ Error: /workspace directory is not successfully mounted!"
  exit 1
else
  echo "✓ Workspace directory mount verified."
fi

# 4. Check for Google Workspace MCP servers in config file
CONFIG_PATH="/home/agy_runner/.gemini/config/mcp_config.json"
if ! podman exec agy-sandbox cat "$CONFIG_PATH" | grep -q "gws-"; then
  echo "❌ Error: Google Workspace MCP configuration not found in mcp_config.json!"
  exit 1
else
  echo "✓ Google Workspace MCP servers detected in mcp_config.json."
fi

# 5. Execute headless dry run check
echo "⚙ Running diagnostic CLI dry run inside the container..."
podman exec agy-sandbox agy --verify --headless

echo "==========================================="
echo "🎉 Verification passed! Ready for certification submission."
echo "==========================================="
Check Your Understanding
Question 1: What is the purpose of mapping the workspace folder to the container as a volume instead of copying files directly into the container image?
A) Volume mapping is faster than building images.
B) Volume mapping allows files edited by the agent inside the container to persist on the host filesystem immediately, maintaining an immutable tool environment.
C) To bypass SSL certificate validation.
D) Docker requires all containers to map volumes.

Correct Answer: B. Decoupling the tool environment (immutable container image) from the work directory (dynamic host volume) allows the agent to safely edit files that persist on the host, without needing to rebuild the image.
Question 2: What is checked by the capstone verification script to ensure pipeline security?
A) The presence of a graphics driver.
B) Container running status, proxy environment variables, workspace volume mount, Google Workspace MCP servers, and successful dry-run validation.
C) Snyk vulnerability scanning logs.
D) Host root partition size.
Correct Answer: B. The validation script checks container status, proxy mapping, workspace mounts, config validity, and CLI status checks to confirm environment readiness.
